name: Package Management
summary: Distribution-level identity, versioning, dependency resolution, installation, and build orchestration above the source-language module layer; modern designs span content-hashed lockfiles, scoped namespaces, hermetic builds (Nix flakes), Sigstore-signed provenance, and ecosystem-scale rebuild verification (OSS Rebuild).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Package management operates above the source-language module layer and deals with distribution, versioning, installation, dependency graphs, and build orchestration. It matters because many language ecosystems are shaped as much by their package tooling as by the language itself.

Three distinct concepts often blur together: **source-level module identity** (the names programmers write in imports), **build-graph identity** (the units the compiler actually schedules and caches), and **distribution identity** (the units versioned, downloaded, published, and installed). Some systems align these cleanly; some intentionally keep them separate; some make them partially overlap, which often creates the most confusion.

sections.title: Module identity vs package identity
sections.content:
Some ecosystems tightly couple import identity and package identity, while others keep them layered or loosely related. This design choice shapes how developers think about source structure, dependency resolution, and reproducibility.

Four broad postures emerge: **layered but explicit** (Rust: package, crate, and module are distinct and named separately); **closely aligned** (Go: module path strongly shapes package import identity, with Semantic Import Versioning encoding major version into the module path itself for v2+); **loosely coupled** (Python: import system and packaging ecosystem are distinct layers); **metadata-shaped resolution** (Node: package.json `"exports"`, `"type"`, and conditional exports actively participate in import semantics).

Deno demonstrates a fifth: **URL-shaped identity with no central registry** — module identity *is* an HTTPS URL, content-addressed via lockfile, with no governance bottleneck. Go's import-paths-as-URLs takes a similar position with a less radical lockfile model.

sections.title: Build orchestration — lockfiles, workspaces, registries
sections.content:
Package managers often evolve into build tools, test runners, and publication orchestrators. This integration can improve coherence and usability, but it can also increase coupling between language semantics and ecosystem workflow assumptions.

**Lockfiles with content hashes are now table stakes**. The dominant lockfile families are `Cargo.lock` (Rust, TOML), `package-lock.json` / `yarn.lock` / `pnpm-lock.yaml` (Node), `go.sum` (Go, validated against `sum.golang.org`), `Pipfile.lock` / `poetry.lock` / `uv.lock` (Python). Go's checksum database goes further by maintaining a public log of every known module version's hash, defending against retroactive registry changes.

**Workspaces** are the package-manager-level answer to "I have several libraries that evolve together." Cargo workspaces (root `Cargo.toml` with `[workspace]` and `members`), Go workspaces (`go.work`, March 2022), and pnpm/Yarn/npm workspaces all give monorepo benefits while preserving per-package independence.

**Public registries**: crates.io, npm, PyPI, Hex, Hackage, NuGet, Maven Central. Go modules has *no central registry* — module paths are URLs (typically `github.com/user/repo`), the Go tool fetches directly from the version-control host or a configured proxy. By making module identity an arbitrary URL-shaped string, Go avoids registry-name-squatting entirely.

sections.title: Supply chain and provenance — Sigstore, SLSA, OSS Rebuild
sections.content:
Lockfiles and content hashes help only after a consumer has decided which artifact to trust. Modern supply-chain tooling addresses an earlier question: *should this artifact have been trusted in the first place?*

**Sigstore** (OpenSSF, 2021+) is the cryptographic substrate. Its design point is **keyless signing via short-lived OIDC-bound certificates** issued by the **Fulcio** certificate authority and recorded in the **Rekor** transparency log. A CI job presents an OIDC identity token to Fulcio, receives a code-signing certificate valid for ~10 minutes, signs the artifact, and submits the signature plus certificate to Rekor. This eliminates long-lived signing keys.

**SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts)** specifies *what* build provenance must include — build-system identity, source revision, dependencies, isolation properties — and assigns Levels 1–4 corresponding to increasingly strong tamper-resistance guarantees.

**cosign** is the canonical CLI tying the two together. Status (as of 2026-04): GitHub Actions emits SLSA Level 3 build provenance via `actions/attest-build-provenance`; npm enforces Sigstore-signed publishing for trusted packages since 2024; PyPI Trusted Publishing uses the same OIDC-to-Sigstore flow.

**Google's OSS Rebuild** (announced 2025) attempts to **rebuild published packages at ecosystem scale** and compare the result with the upstream artifact — continuous, third-party validation of public package ecosystems. The three layers compose end-to-end: a consumer can pin a content-hashed dependency, verify the publisher's identity from its Sigstore signature, inspect the SLSA attestation, and (with OSS Rebuild) independently re-derive the artifact.

**Nix flakes** and **Guix** take an even more aggressive position: every package is a pure function from inputs to outputs, and the build sandbox is hermetic by language-level construction. Two builds against the same `flake.lock` produce identical outputs byte for byte. The trade-off is acquisition cost (significant Nix learning curve) for hermetic reproducibility no lockfile-based system can match.

sections.title: Design implications
sections.content:
Package management should be treated as a first-class ecosystem architecture concern rather than an afterthought. Clear identity models, deterministic resolution, and coherent build integration all influence whether the ecosystem feels stable and predictable to both users and tools.

A defensible default for new ecosystems: keep module identity clear and source-level; keep package identity explicit but not overly entangled with import syntax; commit to scoped registry names from day one (defending against name-squatting); content-hashed lockfiles by default; workspace support as a first-class feature; vendoring as a first-class workflow for hermetic builds; consider whether version identity should ever leak into source import paths.

The **Rust-Based Modern JS Toolchain phenomenon** (2022–2026) — esbuild, swc, oxc, biome, Rolldown, rspack, Turbopack — illustrates that the JavaScript build-tool ecosystem migrated wholesale to Rust within a few years. The lesson generalises: a mature ecosystem can be re-implemented in a faster systems language one tool at a time, with API compatibility as the governing constraint. For language designers shipping a new ecosystem, the choice between "fast tools from day one in a systems language" and "tools written in the language itself" has a clear answer in 2026: ship in a systems language from day 1.
